Q1shman's weakness while a
member of the official board of
kOotenai county was to favor the
Northern Pacific railroad in the
matter of taxation on its lands, of
which the railroad then held large
amount. Dishman voted with the
railroad on every proposition in
which it was interested in in the
two years in which he was in office.
Dishman went into office a compar-
atively poor man and came out with
money He then moved across the
border into Spokane county, bought
land here, changed his politics and
is now referred to, in the campaign
booklet, as lone of our prominent
business men,"


In July, 1898, when again equaliz-
ing the land values for that year,
the only taxpayer save one in Koot-
enai county to kick was the North
ern Pacific, which knew that it had
a friend at court in Dishman. This
man voted to cut the Northern Pa
cific assessment in some instances
from listed valuation of Is cents
per acre to 25 cents per acre. Some
lands were cut from 81 to 75 cents;
five sections from 81 to $3.50 per
acre; and SO sections from $3.50 to
$1.25 per acre. In all, the reduc-
tions granted the Northern Pacific
that year amounted to $9743. Smith,
honest man, went on record
against 1t.


For nearly 10 years the Northern
Pacific had failed to pay Kootenai
county any taxes whatever on its
land holdings, and the land had
been sold for taxes and bought in
by the county. In 1898 fasteners
were coming into the northwest,
prospects were beginning to
brighten and the Northern Pacific
thought that the time had arrived to
regain the title to it's land. They
looked to Dishman and Cable to
turn the trick, and the trick was
turned. Smith, however, was not in
on the play, and called the roll, as
usual.


The back taxes on this land then
amounted to $10,323. The railroad,
with the vote of Dishman, settled
for $4,000, was decided back it's land
and the whole incident closed. The
land was then worth tens of thou
sands of dollars and the railroad
should have lost it the same as any
other of the thousands of private
taxpayers who were sold out for
taxation in the hard times. The PR.
vate taxpayers had no friends on
the board of county commissioners;
the railroad had-there was the diff
ference.
